nutrient_source.title: Vermicelli Salad (ကြာဇံသုတ်) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vermicelli Salad (ကြာဇံသုတ်), CR ID G00010614. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat not directly reported by HPB; estimated at approximately 6.5 g/100g based on typical oil and dried shrimp composition for this dish type, consistent with USDA FoodData Central reference data for similar preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Burmese-style Vermicelli Salad (Kyar Zan Thoke). GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: rice vermicelli / glass noodles, which have a reported GI of approximately 58 per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per 200 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (58 × 11.8) / 100 ≈ 6.8. The very high fat content (44.5 g/100g) is expected to further attenuate the glycemic response in practice.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold; fish sauce and dried shrimp are primary contributors; low potassium (64 mg/100g) provides no meaningful offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, reduce fish sauce and dried shrimp substantially and increase potassium-rich vegetables to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high fat content (44.5 g/100g) and caloric density (430 kcal/100g) with zero dietary fibre impair insulin sensitivity and promote weight gain, both of which are metabolically unfavourable for Type 2 Diabetes management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this dish in its standard preparation; if modified, reduce oil and fried components significantly, increase non-starchy vegetable proportion to add fibre, and limit portion size to minimise glycaemic and caloric impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (44.5 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g/100g, high sodium (810 mg/100g), and cholesterol (26 mg/100g) collectively elevate LDL cholesterol and blood pressure risk, both primary CVD risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish in its standard form; if consumed occasionally, reduce oil, replace fish sauce with low-sodium seasoning, and pair with high-fibre vegetables to partially mitigate the atherogenic lipid and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g exceeds RA threshold; estimated saturated fat ~6.5 g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; chilli (nightshade) present; zero dietary fibre provides no gut microbiome anti-inflammatory benefit.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; nightshade ingredient (chilli) may exacerbate joint inflammation, and the high sodium and saturated fat profile promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vermicelli Salad (ကြာဇံသုတ်, romanized: Kyar Zan Thoke) is a Burmese-origin cold noodle salad dish widely available in Singapore's hawker and food court settings. It is prepared by boiling thin rice or glass vermicelli noodles until tender, then tossing them with a dressing of fish sauce, lime juice, dried shrimp, chilli, fried shallots, garlic, and a generous quantity of cooking oil. The result is a dish with a savoury, tangy, and mildly spicy flavour profile, with a soft and slightly slippery noodle texture contrasted by the crunch of fried shallots. Per 100 g, the dish provides 430 kcal, 44.5 g fat, 1.32 g protein, 5.9 g carbohydrate, and 810 mg sodium. The extremely high fat content is primarily attributable to the large volume of oil used in the dressing and the fried shallot component. Dietary fibre is negligible at 0 g/100g. Clinically, this dish is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (sodium far exceeds safe thresholds), Type 2 Diabetes (high fat, zero fibre, high caloric density), cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (nightshade ingredient, pro-inflammatory fat profile). The glycemic index is estimated at 58 (medium) based on the glass noodle / rice vermicelli analogue, but the very high fat content is expected to attenuate the glycemic response in practice. This dish is commonly served as a lunch or dinner item in Singapore and is associated with Burmese culinary heritage.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID G00010614) with confidence 0.95. All core macros and secondary nutrients are present and internally consistent. Saturated fat estimated from component analysis as HPB does not report it directly; this is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved source (University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al., 2008).
notes: 1. Saturated fat (6.5 g/100g) is estimated based on typical oil and dried shrimp composition for this dish type; HPB does not report saturated fat directly for this entry. Estimation cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central data for similar preparations. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this specific Burmese-style vermicelli salad preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB for this dish; this is a confirmed zero per the approved source, consistent with a preparation dominated by refined vermicelli noodles and oil with minimal vegetable content. 4. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli / glass noodles per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008); no direct GI measurement for Kyar Zan Thoke exists in the approved GI literature. 5. Halal status is uncertain as fish sauce and dried shrimp sourcing is not specified; is_halal set to false as a conservative default. 6. The dish name includes a Burmese-script component (ကြာဇံသုတ်); this is preserved in aliases for retrieval purposes.
